title: Governing the Long Game
subtitle: Short-termism vs the long horizon

hook.metaphor: The hardest thing about a long-range threat is that the people in charge are usually rewarded for the short term. A CEO judged on this year's profit has every incentive to leave the slow storm for their successor. Governing ESG and climate risk is really about beating that short-termism — building a structure that makes the organisation act on a threat whose worst effects land after today's leaders have moved on.
hook.scenario: A board agrees climate is a serious long-term risk — then approves a strategy and bonus plan based purely on the next two years' profit, with no one accountable for the long-horizon risks. They 'believe in' ESG. Why will nothing actually change?

learn: The core tension is **short-termism vs the long horizon.** ESG risks land over decades, but strategy, reporting, and especially **incentives** usually run on quarters and years. If nobody is rewarded for managing the long game (our board's 2-year bonus plan), belief in ESG stays a slogan while behaviour chases the next quarter.

So governance must **embed ESG into strategy and incentives**, not bolt it on. That means board-level oversight of climate/ESG risk, integrating it into the **risk appetite** and **enterprise risk** view (the recurring boardroom ideas), and tying some executive reward to long-term sustainability goals — so the long horizon has a champion with skin in the game.

Clear **accountability** is essential: a named owner (often at board or executive level) for ESG/climate risk, with it on the agenda regularly — not delegated to a sidelined sustainability team with no power (the same independence/authority lesson from every governance level).

It increasingly has regulatory teeth: mandatory climate disclosure, **APRA CPG 229** expectations, central-bank climate stress tests, and director-duty guidance that climate risk is a *financial* risk boards must consider. 'We didn't think it was our job' is no longer a defence for directors.

And the golden thread, one final time: **culture and tone at the top** decide whether ESG is genuine or theatre. A board that truly treats the long game as real — funding it, measuring it, rewarding it, and being honest about trade-offs — turns ESG from a glossy report into actual resilience. One that just 'believes in it' while paying for short-term profit changes nothing at all.
bridge: So at work, you can tell genuine ESG governance from greenwashed good intentions with one test: *does the organisation put structure and money behind the long game — board ownership, ESG in the risk appetite, and incentives that reward long-term outcomes — or does it 'believe in' ESG while paying purely for short-term profit?* The slow storm is only ever managed by beating short-termism, and that's a governance and culture choice.

interactions.title: Belief without change
interactions.prompt: The board 'believes in' climate risk but bases strategy and bonuses purely on 2-year profit, with no long-horizon owner. Why won't anything change?

options.label: It will change — believing in ESG is enough.
options.feedback: Belief without structure changes nothing. If incentives reward only short-term profit and no one owns the long-horizon risk, behaviour keeps chasing the next quarter regardless of stated belief.

options.label: Incentives and accountability still reward short-termism — ESG isn't embedded.
options.feedback: Exactly. Until ESG is in the strategy, the risk appetite, the incentives, and someone's accountability, it stays a slogan. You manage the long game by beating short-termism structurally.

options.label: Just publish a glossier sustainability report.
options.feedback: A nicer report without changed incentives is greenwashing-adjacent theatre. Behaviour follows reward and accountability, not brochures.

interactions.title: Genuine governance or theatre?
interactions.prompt: Sort each practice by whether it embeds ESG or just performs it.

items.label: Tying executive pay partly to long-term sustainability goals
'Believing in' ESG while rewarding only short-term profit
Board-level owner accountable for climate risk
A glossy report with no change to incentives
ESG built into the risk appetite and strategy
A powerless sustainability team kept on the sidelines

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Governing ESG means beating short-termism — embedding the long game in strategy, the risk appetite, accountability, and incentives — because a board that only 'believes in' ESG while paying for short-term profit changes nothing at all.”

checkpoint.q: The central challenge in governing ESG/climate risk is…
options: It's too cheap to bother with
Short-termism — leaders are rewarded for the near term while risks land long-term
checkpoint.explain: Long-horizon risks get neglected when strategy and incentives run on quarters and years.

checkpoint.q: For ESG governance to actually change behaviour, it must be…
options: Embedded in strategy, risk appetite, accountability, and incentives
Delegated to a powerless side team
checkpoint.explain: Behaviour follows reward and ownership — ESG has to be built in, not bolted on.

checkpoint.q: Directors increasingly cannot say 'climate isn't our job' because…
options: Regulators and duty guidance treat climate as a financial risk boards must consider
checkpoint.explain: Mandatory disclosure, CPG 229, and director-duty guidance make climate a board-level financial responsibility.
